to this stuff. So with that in mind, I would have you asked these people or offered them your preferred engagement model, right? Compared to an employee where if 20% of your job sucks, you can't tell your boss, hey, I don't want to do this 20% of my job anymore. You're stuck. Yeah, so the question I had for you was someone that I know that knows that I'm a copywriter put me through to someone who has a business, a financial motivational speaker. It's just sales pages here and there. So it looks like a big project as well. He doesn't have an email see just sends out emails to people, you know, with, you know, uplifting comments, words of the day, that sort of thing. But he has 16,000 people on his list. So the question I have for you is, I mean, I've never taken on anything like this before. So, you know, and it seems like a large project, what would be the cost to him? I've gotten some feedback from other people about what they think the cost should be. But I just want to hear from you and really how I should tackle this because, you know, I haven't really gotten a lot of work in copywriting. So this is one of the first big things and it's pretty big. So just curious from you how to tackle something like this, how to price it out, what kind of deal to structure, strategy and that sort of thing. Especially because this is the first project of this kind of scale for you. I would not do the whole thing at once. I would try to break it down into meaningful milestones where you talk with him, you figure out, you know, do some type of analysis of where his biggest missed opportunity is, try to give him a sense of how much money could, you know, is potentially available if it goes well. I'm assuming that this person, you know, with a 16,000 person email list that they've got some money. But at the same time, with a funnel like this where it's like kind of strung together with duct tape and it may not be converting all that well, you know, you can't go in assuming that this person has a nice budget to work with. So I would look to see what's the biggest opportunity for them to get cash in the door. I would give him a fair, if not a little bit low, initial rate on that first project, both because, you know, you don't have the experience and the expertise to really confidently say, yes, I will get you this exceptional result.

## [45:39 - 48:41]

So being like charging for that upfront on the first project to me is risky, whereas if you go in and say, based on my expertise, this is the biggest number one opportunity, the highest leverage project we could do. Let's tackle that. I will give you a fair rate on that. And if it goes well and it brings in a ton of money, then, you know, on the next project, I will, will charge based on the success of the first project. So that way, you're sort of, you're testing each other out, right? You're allowing him to try you as a fairly new copywriter, just the way you're getting to get some experience on a project that's bigger than the scope you've handled in the past. So, you know, you're gaining experience in exchange, you know, rather than making 100% compensation in money, you know, you may get 60% compensation in money and 40% compensation in experience, but then you turn around and sell that experience back to this same client. And if you've made him a bunch of money in the first project, cost should not be an objection on the next piece. And then you just do it in phases and build it out over time, you know, and part of me wanted to sort of throw out a performance based suggestion if it goes really well, but that just gets way too complicated, especially for somebody who doesn't have that level of sophistication and for it being sort of your first time in this too. So I would say pick a manageable initial scope that's got a big potential for success, give him a fair if not slightly low rate in exchange for getting that experience locked in. If it goes well, there should be no objection to hiring you for the next phase. And on that, that's when you can start to talk about performance. That would be how I would approach it. Any questions? saying, okay, he's got 16,000 people on his list. that. I also am wondering, I'm, I'm, I'm, I feel, I'm feeling stuck and a little frustrated and a little sad, especially after this last one where I was really, really crossing my fingers for. ## [54:47 - 57:51]

back to past clients proposing potential projects or even just checking in and seeing how the copy did. Is that one way to go also, if you have potential clients, rather than jumping right into the retainer, in the back of your mind, you can have a plan like if this goes well, project B will be a retainer or if project A goes well, then we go on to be then we go on to retainer. You know, I know that when you're in this place of frustration, me telling you that it's going to take more time, may not always be the most welcome news. But at the same time, I think that that reactivating past clients, if you can, either with one off projects or retainers, is much more streamlined in terms of your time than trying to find yet another new person to, you know, know, like and trust you and then go through the whole process of signing them and all that, like new clients are kind of the devil, right? If you can either maximize your existing clients or your existing network, rather than having to reach out to brand new, cold people, any number of ways to do that, I think is helpful. And one idea that just popped into my head is the J. Abraham has a thing where you find businesses that are non competitive and sell something that's before, during, or after what you sell and you partner with them to refer work, right? So this is actually how I found my largest retainer client. They're no longer with me, but at the time, over three and a half years, they paid me like $163,000 because I started by working with a friend of mine who had an A.B. testing agency, they were doing a test on this e-commerce company's entire product page. The company sells basically as one more key product that like drives 90% of their business or more. So they were going to test this main product page and they needed a copywriter to actually do the copy. So I came in subcontracted for my friend, our test killed it, boosted conversions by like 15%, which is seven figures a month to this business. And then they were like, who was that copywriter? We would love to work with him on more stuff. And my friend was like, I don't want to be in the middle of this. You talk to them. And boom, I had a retainer. We worked on a number of projects together. And then eventually they were my first retainer and have to date are my biggest retainer, all because of that non competitive partner relationship with my friend. So that's another way if you can think about who are the best businesses hiring before, during, or after the copy? And can you find ways to tap into that so that other people go out and find those clients and then you just become a partner with them to support their, those initial projects. That's not in there. Right. If I didn't have the text, it's much easier to say, I read this, this is a problem. We got to fix it versus this thing that I need is missing.
